coredata.dc:title: Theoretical insight on hybrid nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot and carbazole–carbazole dyes as an efficient sensitizer of DSSC
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Feng
Yang, Chuan-Lu
Wang, Mei-Shan
Ma, Xiao-Guang
Liu, Wen-Wang
coredata.dc:description: The feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of the graphene quantum dot (GQD) and carbazole–carbazole dyes as the efficient sensitizer of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) is investigated. By using the first principles density functional theory (DFT), we fully optimize the geometrical structures of GQD, the carbazole–carbazole dyes, and their hybrid nanocomposites. The harmonic frequency analysis is used to confirm the energy stability of the optimized structures. The optical absorptions of the structures are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT). Using the I−/I3 − electrolyte and the conduction band minimum of TiO2 electrode as a sample, we examine the feasibility of the nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC with the charge spatial separation and the molecular orbital energy levels of the nanocomposites. The results demonstrate all the considered nanocomposites have suitable energy levels of the frontier orbitals and significantly charge spatial separation. TDDFT results show the oscillator strengths of all nanocomposites demonstrate the obvious enhancement in the visible light region. Moreover, the appropriate open-circuit voltage value, the larger light-harvesting efficiency, and larger driving force are also identified for these nanocomposites. Therefore, the nanocomposites could be the more promising candidates of sensitizer for DSSC in comparison with the separate carbazole–carbazole dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensitizer
Nanocomposites
Charge spatial separation
Energy levels
Optical absorption

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structures of the carbazole–carbazole dyes and the nanocomposites.
The vibrational spectra for the nanocomposites comprising of GQD and carbazole–carbazole dyes, calculated at B3LYP-6-31G (d, p).
The energy levels of HOMO and LUMO the nanocomposites match with CBMs of the TiO2 electrode and the I−/I3 − electrolyte. All are calculated at B3LYP/6-31G (d, p)/PCM-THF.
The calculated HOMO and LUMO charge distributions of the nanocomposites at the B3LYP/6-3G (d, p)/PCM-THF.
The calculated absorption spectra at PCM-TD-CAM-B3LYP/6-311 + G (d, p) with THF for the nanocomposites and the separate GQD and the carbazole–carbazole dyes.

The calculated main electron transitions, maximum absorption wavelengths (λ max), oscillator strengths (f), and excitation energies of the carbazole–carbazole dyes and the nanocomposites in THF solvent at TD-PCM-CAM-B3LYP/6–311+ G (d, p).
The calculated E ox nan∗ , ∆G injec, LHE, and V oc of carbazole–carbazole dyes and the nanocomposites calculated at TD-CAM-B3LYP/6–311 + G (d, p).

head.ce:title: Theoretical insight on hybrid nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot and carbazole–carbazole dyes as an efficient sensitizer of DSSC

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronics Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronics Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of the graphene quantum dot (GQD) and carbazole–carbazole dyes as the efficient sensitizer of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) is investigated. By using the first principles density functional theory (DFT), we fully optimize the geometrical structures of GQD, the carbazole–carbazole dyes, and their hybrid nanocomposites. The harmonic frequency analysis is used to confirm the energy stability of the optimized structures. The optical absorptions of the structures are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT). Using the I−/I3 − electrolyte and the conduction band minimum of TiO2 electrode as a sample, we examine the feasibility of the nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC with the charge spatial separation and the molecular orbital energy levels of the nanocomposites. The results demonstrate all the considered nanocomposites have suitable energy levels of the frontier orbitals and significantly charge spatial separation. TDDFT results show the oscillator strengths of all nanocomposites demonstrate the obvious enhancement in the visible light region. Moreover, the appropriate open-circuit voltage value, the larger light-harvesting efficiency, and larger driving force are also identified for these nanocomposites. Therefore, the nanocomposites could be the more promising candidates of sensitizer for DSSC in comparison with the separate carbazole–carbazole dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: The exploration of renewable and environmentally friendly energy sources is becoming a global interest motif due to the long-term shortage issues of fossil fuels and serious environmental issues. Since ÓRegan and Grätzel reported a significant breakthrough work about solar energy harvesting and power conversion efficiency in 1991 [1], the dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) has received too much attention as the most promising alternative to the traditional silicon based solar cells owing to their versatility, easy production, cost effective, and relatively high energy conversion efficiency [2–6]. Usually, the function of DSSC mainly depends on the sensitizer which attached to the surfaces of the electrode of the oxide semiconductor. The sensitizer plays a vital role in the light-harvesting efficiency. As an efficient sensitizer, it must harvest the solar energy to excite and inject electrons in the electrode according to the work flow of DSSC [7]. The effective sensitizer for DSSC is very important to improve the overall efficiency of the cell. In recent years, many theoretical and experimental studies have focused on designing novel sensitizer to improve DSSC performance [8].

The efficiency of the sensitizer is well known to depend strongly on its chemical structure. Generally, most highly efficient and typical dye sensitizer consisted of a donor, π-conjugation bridge, and acceptor moieties, that is, the D–π–A structure [9–11]. This push–pull system induces the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) from donor to acceptor part through the π-conjugation bridge, which makes the electron injected into the conduction band minimum (CBM) of the electrode, which is important for light harvesting. Moreover, more and more researchers find that D–D–π–A structure shows superior performance over the simple D–π–A structure, in terms of the better extended absorption spectra, molecular extinction coefficient, and thermal stability. Among the wide range of D–D–π–A structures being investigated for application in DSSC, carbazole–carbazole is one of the best electron donor moieties for D–D–π–A system [12–15]. Recently, Koumura et al. [16] have reported a series of the metal-free organic dyes based on carbazole–carbazole unit as an electron donor and cyanoacrylic acid as an electron acceptor for D–D–π–A system. The results show that the two carbazole–carbazole dyes containing thieno [3,2-b] thiophene and benzothiadiazole as the π-linker exhibits high performance. However, the optical absorption of some carbazole–carbazole dyes is not obvious in the visible light range. Such as the single π-linker unit with different groups for furan (CCFA), pyrrole (CCPyA), and phenyl (CCPhA). Although these carbazole–carbazole dyes satisfy the requirement of the electronic structures, frontier molecular orbitals, and energy levels as a sensitizer of DSSC, they are less efficient because they have no obvious absorption in the most part of the visible light region. To improve the efficiency of carbazole–carbazole dyes as the sensitizer of DSSC, one could enhance the optical absorption of these structures in the visible light range by modifying the structures with some functional ligands or hybridizing quantum dots (QD). In fact, the sensitizer of nanocomposite proves to be efficient to harvest solar energy, because the nanocomposite can have superior properties as compared to the individual components from which it is made [17,18]. Among the various QDs, graphene quantum dot (GQD) demonstrated most of the excellent performances and optical properties, which makes them a potential candidate for the fabrication of nanocomposite with favorable properties. Unlike the bulk graphene, GQD has a band gap because of quantum confinement [19,20] and exhibit strong photoluminescence (PL) [21,22]. The nanocomposite consisting of GQD and phenoxazine-based dyes with D–π–A structure are obvious optical absorption enhancement effect than the single phenoxazine-based dyes and identified as the favored candidate of the sensitizer for DSSC [23].

Considering that carbazole–carbazole dyes satisfy the requirement sensitizer of DSSC but their light absorptions are very poor in the light visible region, we have constructed several nanocomposites of GQD and carbazole-carbazole dyes. Then we perform density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations on the geometries, electronic structures, and absorption spectra of all the nanocomposites. The key physical quantities, such as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap (E g), spatial charge separation, light-harvesting efficiency (LHE), free energy change (ΔG inject) for electron injection, and the open-circuit voltage (V oc) of photovoltaic device are evaluated. The results demonstrated that the absorption spectra of the nanocomposites are obvious widening and the oscillator strengths are significantly enhanced while the nanocomposites still satisfy the requirement for the sensitizer of DSSC. These findings can provide a helpful guide for developing efficient nanocomposites dyes sensitizer for DSSC.

In order to find the better ones of GQD-D–D–π–A nanocomposites for sensitizers, we designed the structures of nanocomposites including GQD-CCFA, GQD-CCPyA, GQD-CCPhA. In addition, to explore the coupling effect of GQD and different dye molecules in more detail, we also studied the other carbazole–carbazole dyes mentioned in Koumura et al. [16], such as the π-linker with different groups including forbenzothiadizole (CCBA), thienothiophene (CCThA), and thiophene (CCTA) dyes. The considered structures of GQD, all designed carbazole–carbazole dyes and nanocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The cir-coronene C54H18 structure is used to stand for the present GQD according to the detail discussion about the structural parameters and optical properties by Li et al. [24]. The geometrical structures of the nanocomposites are constructed by attaching the carbazole–carbazole dyes with the GQD through an amide linkage. As a result, the carbazole–carbazole dye moiety is attached to the GQD through a single CC linkage. The geometrical configurations of the separate GQD, carbazole–carbazole dyes and their hybridized nanocomposites are optimized by using the hybrid DFT method as implemented in the Gaussian 09 program [25]. The choice of the exchange correlation functional is important for an accurate description of the charge transfer and electronic properties at the ground and excited state. The B3LYP [26,27] method with 6-31G (d, p) basis set is used. The calculation level is the same as that of Koumura et al. [16]. B3LYP is a gradient-corrected hybrid DFT with exchange-correlation functional in Becke's three-parameter form, which includes a mixture of Hartree-Fock (HF) exchange with the Vosko, Wilk, and Nusair (VWN) functional III for local correlation and the correlation functional of Lee, Yang, and Parr (LYP) for non-local correlation. The polarizable continuum model (PCM) [28] with the solvent effect of tetrahydrofuran (THF) is also used in the geometrical structure optimization and TDDFT calculations. The default convergent criteria of Gaussian 09 are used for the energy and force constants. Based on the optimized geometries, the vertical excitation energy and electronic absorption spectra are calculated by TDDFT methods. We use 40 electronic states for all structures and nanocomposites. The absorption spectra for all nanocomposites are calculated at long-range corrected hybrid CAM-B3LYP [29] functionals, with a larger basis set of 6–311 + G (d, p).

The overall energy conversion efficiency (η) of DSSC depends on the short-circuit current density (J sc), the V oc, and the fill factor (FF) according to previous investigations [30–32]. The V oc can be calculated based on electron injection from the LUMO level of sensitizer to the CBM of the electrode (E CBM) [33].
(1)
V oc
=
E LUMO
−
E CBM
e

A high J sc value is associated with a strong light absorption capability and high electron injection efficiency (Φ inject) from the excited sensitizer to the E CBM of the electrode. J sc as expressed in Eq. (2) [34], which is proportional to the LHE at the maximum absorption wavelength, Φ inject is the electron injection efficiency, and η collect is the charge collection efficiency. Therefore, the sensitizer with higher LHE and Φ inject inject values should possess higher J sc.
(2)
J sc
= ∫ LHE
λ
Φ inject
η collect
d λ

Based on Beer's law, LHE [35,36] is approximated by.
(3)
LHE = 1 –
10
− ƒ
where f is the absorption intensity (oscillator strength) of the sensitizer at the maximum wavelength. Φ inject is related to the free energy change ΔG inject (in eV) for electron injection onto the surface of the electrode that can be obtained from Eq. (4) [37]. Where E ox nan∗ represents the photoinduced excited states of the reduction potentials and E CBM represents the reduction potential of the conduction band of the electrode. The E ox nan∗ can be estimated by using Eq. (5), where E ox is the redox potential of the nanocomposites in the ground state and E 0–0 is the vertical transition energy.
(4)
Δ
G injec

E ox
nan ∗

E CBM
(5)

E
0 − 0

The optimized geometrical structures of all the nanocomposites comprising of GQD and carbazole–carbazole dyes are shown in Fig. 1(b). To access the energy and chemical stabilities of these structures, we calculate the harmonic frequencies and the E
g between HOMO and LUMO for them. Table 1 collects the range of the harmonic frequencies, HOMO, LUMO, E
g for all the considered structures. Fortunately, all the structures are stable in energy because no imaginary harmonic frequencies are found. Table 1 demonstrates that the present E
g of GQD is 2.824 eV at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) theoretical level, which is larger than the 1.96 eV with common DFT but smaller than the 4.68 eV with GW method [24]. The E
g s of carbazole–carbazole dyes from 1.807 to 2.896 eV, which is roughly the same as those of Koumura et al. [16]. The E
g s of the nanocomposites are from 1.432 to 2.528 eV, which means that they are moderate chemical stability. All the E
g s of the nanocomposites are smaller than the separated carbazole–carbazole dyes and GQD, which may result in the red-shift in its absorption spectra of the nanocomposites. We illustrate the vibrational spectra in Fig. 2 to demonstrate them visually. The figure shows that the vibrational spectra of all the nanocomposites are positive, which confirms the energy stability of the nanocomposites. One can find from the figure that the vibrational absorptions of all the nanocomposites are mainly in the near-infrared region, which implies the sizes of the nanocomposites bring about a little variation of the vibrational spectra. In addition, the nanocomposites can also be used to harvest the near-infrared region energy of the sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: As a satisfactory sensitizer of DSSC, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the nanocomposites must match to the energy level of the electrolyte and the band energy of the electrode; otherwise, the electrons cannot transfer from the electrolyte to the LUMO of sensitizer and to the electrode. Here, the electrolyte of the pair I−/I3 − and the TiO2 electrode are used as the example to demonstrate the electron transferring and to examine the feasibility of the present nanocomposites as the sensitizer. Normally, it is desirable that the LUMO of the sensitizer locates above the CBM of TiO2 (−4.3 eV) [38], which will ensure the rapid injection of an electron from the sensitizer to the electrode. Meanwhile, the HOMO should be below the redox energy level of I−/I3 − (around −4.80 eV) [39,40], which promises the efficient regeneration of the sensitizer. We demonstrate the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO for all the considered nanocomposites and the CBM of TiO2 and the redox energy level of I−/I3 − in Fig. 3 . The figure shows that the energy levels of LUMO for all the considered nanocomposites are higher than the CBM of TiO2 and those of HOMO are lower than the redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte. For example, the LUMO energy level of −3.77 eV for GQD-CCBA is obviously higher than the CBM of −4.30 eV for TiO2, which clearly indicates that the electrons can be injected into the electrode effectively. Moreover, the HOMO energy level of −5.21 eV for GQD-CCBA is more negative than the redox energy level of −4.80 eV for I−/I3 − electrolyte.

Beside the HOMO and LUMO levels, the spatial separation of the charge carriers on the sensitizer and the efficient electron transferring from the electrolyte to the sensitizer more factors can also impact the efficiency of DSSC significantly. For instance, the effective charge spatial separation can lower the electron-hole recombination rate. Therefore, the obvious spatial charge separation, as a consequence of that, the low chance of recombination of charge carriers is the key factor in the efficiency of a sensitizer. To demonstrate the spatial charge separation as well as the contributor and acceptor of the absorption transitions, we plot the distributions of HOMO and LUMO for the nanocomposites in Fig. 4 . The figure clearly indicates that the GQD is the contributor of the electron for the optical absorption because the HOMOs of all nanocomposites are localized on GQD. Meanwhile, the LUMOs are localized at dyes as the acceptor. Therefore, all the nanocomposites demonstrate obvious spatial charge separation. As a result, it is safety to say that all nanocomposites are suitable for sensitizer of DSSC.

The optical properties are also an important index of the efficiency of DSSC. A strong optical absorption in the visible light range is necessary to excite the electrons with sunlight. To access the absorption ability of the considered structures, we calculated the absorption spectra of all structures by TD-CAM-B3LYP/6–311 + G (d, p). The calculated ƒ of all structures are presented in Fig. 5 , and the important data are listed in Table 2 . The table shows that all the nanocomposites have higher ƒ than those of the separate carbazole–carbazole dyes in the visible light range. The useful change of the optical absorptions of the nanocomposites is due to the energy variation of LUMOs resulted from the hybrid interaction between GQD and dyes. In Fig. 5, an obvious change is observed for the optical absorption of all nanocomposites in comparison with those of the separate GQD or dye molecules. The absorption peaks of the nanocomposites show obvious red-shift in comparison with that of GQD and have moved into the visible light region. The enhancement absorptions result from the multiple effects of the GQD and carbazole–carbazole dyes. For all the six nanocomposites, the optical absorptions of both the carbazole–carbazole dyes and GQD occur obvious peaks near the wavelength of 500 nm although they are much different in the other wavelength regions. For example, the absorption of GQD falls to zero at about 550 nm while those of CCPhA and CCTA almost reach maximum values. On the whole, the absorptions of the carbazole–carbazole dyes in the visible light region are strengthened by that of GQD. In addition, the positions of the strong absorption of nanocomposites are just located at the positions of the weak absorption of GQD, which indicating an obvious coupling interaction between GQD and dyes. That is, the absorptions of the nanocomposites are not a simple addition of the absorptions of GQD and dyes. This finding also implies that all nanocomposites can effectively respond the solar light and should be the promising candidates to design high-efficiency materials for harvesting solar energy.

E ox nan∗ , ∆G injec, LHE, and V oc of the nanocomposites are calculated and collected in Table 3 to evaluate the electronic dynamics in the transferring process. The results support that the nanocomposites are suitable for sensitizer of DSSC. For examples, all the ∆G injecs of nanocomposites are higher than those of the corresponding dye included in the nanocomposites. Therefore, the nanocomposites have a higher driving force of the injection electron than those of the separate dye, which means that the nanocomposite more spontaneously injects its excited electrons to the CBM of TiO2. The lower E ox nan∗ and higher ∆G injec than those of the corresponding dyes are also identified for the nanocomposites. The larger the value of ∆G injec can bring about the easer charge transferring between the CBM of nanocomposite and the electrolyte. We also find that the LHE values of all nanocomposites are higher than those of the corresponding carbazole–carbazole dyes. Meanwhile, all the V ocs of nanocomposites are slightly lower than those of the corresponding carbazole–carbazole dyes. Therefore, both LHE and V oc of the nanocomposites are suitable for the better performance than the separate carbazole–carbazole dyes as the sensitizer of DSSC.

By employing B3LYP/6-31G (d, p) theoretical level, we have optimized the geometrical structures and calculated electronic properties of eight nanocomposites comprising of GQD with carbazole–carbazole dyes. The geometrical stability of the nanocomposites is confirmed with the harmonic frequency analysis. Based on the optimized structure, we also obtained the optical absorption properties by using TDDFT with CAM-B3LYP/6-311 + G (d, p). The results indicate that all the nanocomposites are stable in energy and have moderate chemical stability. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of all nanocomposites match to the requirements of the typical DSSC with TiO2 electrode and I−/I3 − electrolyte. In addition, the electron and hole recombination of the nanocomposites are slow because they all demonstrate obvious spatial charge separation, which also supports the nanocomposites as a sensitizer of DSSC. The present results with TDDFT demonstrate that the f of all nanocomposites are significantly enhanced in the visible and near infrared ranges, implying they are suitable for DSSC in the view of harvesting solar energy. All the presently considered viewpoints support that the nanocomposites are more suitable for sensitizer of DSSC in comparison with the separate GQD or carbazole–carbazole dyes. These findings are helpful for the design efficient sensitizer of DSSC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant No. 11574125 and No. NSFC-11874192, as well as the Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (ts201511055).
